topics.title: Java Servlets
topics.description: A Servlet is a Java class that runs inside a web container (Tomcat, Jetty) and handles HTTP requests/responses. The container manages the servlet lifecycle: load → instantiate → init() → service() → destroy(). service() dispatches to doGet(), doPost(), doPut(), doDelete() based on HTTP method. Servlets are thread-safe if they hold no mutable instance state — the container reuses a single instance across concurrent requests. HttpSession provides server-side session storage keyed by a session ID cookie (JSESSIONID).
topics.story: You own a restaurant. The web container is your maitre d'. Each incoming HTTP request is a customer. The servlet is a chef — one chef handles all the tables (one instance, many threads). The maitre d' greets each customer (container calls service()), checks their reservation type (GET? POST?), and routes them to the right chef method (doGet/doPost). When the chef first arrives for the shift, they set up their station (init()). When the restaurant closes, they clean up (destroy()). JSESSIONID is the customer's coat-check ticket — lose it and the server forgets who you are.
topics.analogy: Servlet = a telephone operator (one person, many parallel calls). init() = they sit down at the switchboard once. service() = they answer each call and route it. doGet() = reading information, doPost() = changing something on your account. HttpSession = a locker the operator assigns you; the locker number is your JSESSIONID cookie. RequestDispatcher.forward() = transferring the call to another department. response.sendRedirect() = telling the caller to dial a different number themselves.
topics.memoryAnchor: Lifecycle: no-arg constructor → init(ServletConfig) → service(req,res) → destroy(). doGet() for reads, doPost() for writes. HttpServletRequest: getParameter(), getSession(), getHeader(). HttpServletResponse: setContentType(), getWriter(), sendRedirect(). HttpSession: getAttribute(), setAttribute(), invalidate(). @WebServlet("/path") annotation or web.xml mapping. Servlet is NOT thread-safe if it has instance fields — use local variables only. RequestDispatcher rd = request.getRequestDispatcher("/view.jsp"); rd.forward(req, res).
topics.firstPrinciples: HTTP is stateless — each request carries no memory of previous ones. Servlets are stateful holders in a stateless protocol. The container maintains a thread pool; service() runs in a thread from that pool. Instance variables are shared across threads — always use local variables or synchronize. Session is stored on the server; the client holds only the session ID (cookie or URL rewriting). Cookies are max 4KB and sent on every request — don't store sensitive data in them. Sessions are server-side but consume memory — always invalidate on logout.
topics.bruteForce: Static HTML files: no dynamic content, no user-specific pages. CGI scripts: a new process per request — catastrophic at scale. Servlet with instance fields storing request data: race conditions, corrupted responses under concurrent load. Storing full objects in cookies: 4KB limit, security risk, sent on every request.
topics.optimizedApproach: Single servlet instance with only local variables in service methods — thread-safe by design. Connection pooling via JNDI DataSource — never create JDBC connections inside doGet/doPost. Session attributes for user state, not cookies — only session ID in cookie. For complex apps: Servlet as controller, JSP as view (MVC), service classes for business logic. Use Filter for cross-cutting: authentication, logging, CORS. Async servlets (AsyncContext) for long-polling without blocking a thread.
topics.whenToUse: Servlets directly: lightweight endpoints, file downloads, custom binary protocols, filter chains. In practice: Spring MVC (DispatcherServlet), Spring Boot — you rarely write raw servlets, but understanding the lifecycle is essential for debugging Tomcat startup issues, filter ordering, and multipart config. Filters: authentication guards, request logging, GZIP compression, CORS headers.

examples.title: Basic Servlet — doGet, doPost, Session Management
examples.explanation: Three key patterns here: (1) GET renders the form, POST processes it — separating read from write. (2) Session fixation prevention: invalidate the old session before creating a new one on login. (3) PRG (Post-Redirect-Get) pattern: after POST, redirect so browser refresh doesn't re-submit. The Filter intercepts all /dashboard/* requests before the servlet runs — centralized auth check without copy-pasting into every servlet.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring MVC's DispatcherServlet is one giant servlet that delegates to @Controllers — understanding doGet/doPost explains how Spring routes requests. Spring Security's FilterChainProxy is a chain of Filters exactly like AuthFilter above. HttpSession is replaced by Spring Session (Redis-backed) in clustered apps. Servlet multipart config (for file upload) is still configured at the servlet container level even in Spring Boot.

examples.title: Cookie vs Session — When to Use Which
examples.explanation: Cookie vs Session is one of the most common interview questions. Key rule: cookies are client-side (max 4KB, sent automatically, never put secrets there). Sessions are server-side (any size, only the ID travels). Setting HttpOnly prevents XSS attacks from stealing the cookie via JavaScript. Setting Secure ensures it only travels over HTTPS. Invalidating the session on logout is mandatory — a leaked JSESSIONID allows session hijacking.
examples.realWorldUse: JWT tokens replace sessions in stateless APIs — the token travels in Authorization header or cookie, with no server-side storage. Spring Security stores auth in SecurityContextHolder bound to the session by default. Spring Session replaces in-memory session with Redis for horizontal scaling — the JSESSIONID still works, but the session data lives in Redis. 'Remember me' features use persistent cookies with a secure token checked against a DB.

problems.title: Servlet Lifecycle Sequence
problems.hint1: The container creates ONE servlet instance regardless of how many requests come in.
problems.hint2: init() runs once after instantiation. service() runs per request (possibly concurrently). destroy() runs once on shutdown.
problems.hint3: Two concurrent requests = two threads calling service()/doGet() simultaneously on the SAME instance. This is why instance fields are dangerous.
problems.testCases:
Startup: constructor → init
Per-request: service → doGet (concurrent threads, same instance)
Shutdown: destroy
One constructor: Singleton — thread pool reuses the instance
problems.sampleOutput:
Hello, Alice!

; init() called once at startup, service() called per request

problems.title: Thread-Safe vs Unsafe Servlet
problems.hint1: Instance fields are shared across all threads. Local variables live on the thread's stack.
problems.hint2: Thread-1 sets currentUser='Alice'. Thread-2 sets currentUser='Bob'. Thread-1 reads 'Bob'. Alice gets Bob's data.
problems.hint3: Fix: never use instance fields for per-request state. Use local variables, request attributes (req.setAttribute), or session.
problems.testCases:
[UNSAFE] may print wrong username
[SAFE]   always prints correct username
Rule: use local variables in servlet methods
problems.sampleOutput: Unsafe: final count < 100 (lost updates). Safe (AtomicInteger): exactly 100
